en.extract:
The copper pheasant (Syrmaticus soemmerringii), also known as Soemmerring's pheasant or yamadori (ヤマドリ),  is a pheasant endemic to the Japanese archipelago. The scientific name commemorates the German scientist Samuel Thomas von Sömmerring. It is the official bird of multiple Japanese prefectures, cities, and towns. It was commonly hunted for sport throughout the 20th century.
Its population has been in consistent decline since the 1970s due to factors including habitat destruction and predation, but the most widely cited cause is overhunting. 


Taxonomy

The copper pheasant was described in 1830 by Coenraad Temmerick as Phasianus soemmerringii. It was named for the German scientist Samuel Thomas von Sömmerring, while the subspecies S. s. ijimae is named for Japanese zoologist Isao Ijima. It was reclassified into the Syrmaticus genus in 1914 by William Beebe, despite the differences between the males, because he saw a high number of similarities between females of the genus. However, based on the reports of interbreeding between the copper pheasant and the green pheasant (Phasianus versicolor) in Nobusuke Takatsukasa's 1943 pamphlet, Studies of the Galli of Nippon,  the Ornithological Society of Japan moved the bird back into the Phasianus genus. In the same book, Takatsukasa also split the copper pheasant into two species and eliminated all subspecies. Those, as well as other taxonomic changes he proposed, were termed by a United States Fish and Wildlife Service report as "not in accordance with sound systematic practice". The copper pheasant is sometimes still referred to as Phasianus soemmerringii in Japanese literature.
There are five recognized subspecies of copper pheasant:

Soermmering's copper pheasant (S. s. soemmerringii) (Temminck, 1830) is found the northern and central Kyushu. The nominate subspecies, it is dark reddish in colour with an amber sheen to the rump and tips of the upper-tail coverts.
Ijima copper pheasant (S. s. ijimae) (Dresser, 1902), also known as the koshijiro-yamadori (コシジロヤマドリ) (literally white-waisted yamadori), is found in southern to central Kyushu. It is similar in appearance to soemmeringii, but the male has a white rump.
Scintillating copper pheasant (S. s. scintillans) (Gould, 1866), also known as the shining copper pheasant or Honda copper pheasant is found in northern Honshu, from the Kansai region to Aomori prefecture. It is the palest subspecies, with broad white fringes on its belly and flanks.
Shikoku copper pheasant (S. s. intermedius) (Kuroda, 1919) is found in Shikoku and the Chūgoku regions. Like scintillans, it has white fringes on its belly and flanks, but is darker.
Pacific copper pheasant (S. s. subrufus) (Kuroda, 1919) is found in the Ehime, and Kōchi, and Yamaguchi prefectures and the Bōsō, Izu, and Kii peninsula. It has golden fringes on its rump and upper-tail coverts.
Both S. s. ijimae and S. s. scintillans were initially described as distinct species.
Pleistocene-era Syrmaticus fossils in Japan have been classified as S. soemmerringii, but this identification is disputed.


Description
It is a large pheasant with a rich coppery chestnut plumage, yellowish bill, brown iris, and red facial skin. The female is a brown bird with greyish brown upperparts and buff barred dark brown below. The male has short spurs on its grey legs, while the female has none. Males are between 87.5 centimetres (34.4 in) and 136 centimetres (54 in) long, including the tail, while the female is between 51 centimetres (20 in) and 54 centimetres (21 in) (subspecies scintillating copper pheasant, scintillans). Juveniles have similar plumage to the females. The plumage of the chicks is, overall, chestnut, with some darker patches of brown on the back of the neck and cream-coloured feathers on the face. Darker stripes of cream and dark brown run along their face and neck.


Distribution and habitat
The copper pheasant is distributed in and endemic to the hill and mountain forests of the Honshū, Kyūshū, and Shikoku islands at elevations of up to 1,800 metres (1.1 mi). It typically confines itself to deciduous forest floors with extensive vegetation. However, members of the species also lives in conifer forests. They often nest on the edges of grasslands. It naturally occurs only south of the Blakiston's Line, and this species' distribution was originally cited as evidence for the line's existence.
Copper pheasant populations have been established on Hokkaido and Sado Island.


Behavior and ecology
The bird is not heavily vocal, only occasionally making a "kuk-kuk"-like call when breeding or threatened. These pheasants are known to sunbathe, especially when living in grasslands. They roost in trees, and are a non-migratory bird.


Breeding

The copper pheasant's mating season occurs between March and July, with the exact month depending local climate. During this time, males perform their wing-whirring display and fight other males in an attempt to secure a mate. It is unknown if the species is monogamous. Male copper pheasants exhibit aggressive behaviour in captivity, sometimes killing their mates. It is usual for this species to be bred in captivity by means of artificial insemination.
Copper pheasants typically nest on the ground, but have also been recorded nesting in trees. They often build their nests in hollows sheltered by fallen trees. Captive birds have a clutch of ten to twenty eggs, while wild birds have a clutch of seven to thirteen eggs. The cream-coloured or tan eggs take twenty five days to hatch, and the chicks are able to walk within hours of hatching. After all the chicks have hatched, they abandon the nest and stay at their mother's side until adulthood. Occasionally, the male pheasant is seen near his offspring and mate, but young are raised primarily by their mother. When threatened, she will either attack potential predators or attempt to distract them by feigning injury herself. Young birds reach maturity in less than a year.


Food and feeding
Their diet consists mainly of insects, insect larvae, worms, crabs, berries, and acorns, especially those from the Castanopsis, Machilus, Cleyera, and Castanea trees. Adults eat a substantial amount of leaf and fern matter,  while the young mostly consume insects.


Threats
The copper pheasant serves as an important source of prey to Japanese populations of the golden eagle and Hodgson's hawk eagle.  It is susceptible to infection by the heterakis gallinarum parasite.


Relationship to humans


In Japan

The copper pheasant can take the role of a yōkai in Japanese folklore. Tying the pheasant's tail feather's to an arrow grants the arrow the ability to harm malignant spirits in some stories.
The copper pheasant is used in an early 8th century poem by Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, where it is used a metaphor for "the separation of lovers".

葦引の山鳥の尾のしだり尾の     ながながし夜をひとりかも寝むashihiki no yamadori no o no shidari o no naganaga shi yo o hitori kamo nemuAm I to sleep alone this long night, long like a drooping tail, tail feathers of copper pheasants resting mountains apart?(Kakinomoto no Hitomaro)
Several screens, dating to the eighth century and incorporating copper pheasant feathers, are kept in the Shōsō-in temple. The screens were mistakenly assumed to be Chinese, but are, in reality, of Japanese origin. Unidentified bones of either copper pheasants or chickens have been found at Japanese archaeological sites from the Kofun period.
The copper pheasant is a popular game animal in Japan. They are hunted both as a source of food and a source of entertainment.
It is the official bird of the Gunma and Akita prefectures. The Ijima subspecies is the official bird of the Miyazaki prefecture, as well as a natural monument in the town of Asagiri, Kumamoto. As of 2024, the copper pheasant is the also the official bird of Japanese cities, towns, and villages of Hachimantai, Higashinaruse, Ichinohe, Ikawa, Iwaizumi, Isehara, Kamiyama, Kanegasaki,  Karumai, Kitaaiki, Kunohe, Kuzumaki, Mihara, Mogami, Ōkura, Okutama, Shiiba, Sumita, Takayama, Tanohata, Tōno, Yasuoka, Yamakita, and Yuzawa.
It has been depicted on stamps from Japan and Laos.


Outside Japan
Outside of Japan, the copper pheasant is infrequently kept as a pet or display animal.
The Importation of Plumage (No. 1) Order 1922 (SR&O 1922/275) added an addendum to the Importation of Plumage (Prohibition) Act 1921 (11 & 12 Geo. 5. c. 16) which halted the importation of copper pheasant feathers into the UK. They were removed from the list after a year by the Importation of Plumage (No. 2) Order 1923 (SR&O 1923/1496).
From 1907 to 1914, there was a series unsuccessful attempts made by the Hawaii Board of Agriculture and Forestry to establish copper pheasants colonies on the Hawaiian islands. Another attempt to introduce the birds to the United States was made around 1885 by an American diplomat, when he released three pairs of birds into Puget Sound. It was made illegal in the U.S. states of Nebraska and Oregon to hunt birds he had introduced, but a copper pheasant population was never established.


Status
As of 2025, the copper pheasant was listed as "Least Concern" on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species, with an estimated population of 50,000 – 300,000 mature individuals. Prior to this, the species had been listed as Near Threatened since 2004. The ijimae subspecies is protected in Japan, with both S. s. soemmeringii and S. s. ijimae being listed as "Near Threatened" on the Japanese Red List.
In 1995, an IUCN report called for the continued monitoring of the species, citing over-hunting and a lack of knowledge about the species' behaviour as a reason to study and protect them. Threats to the species include non-native predation, overhunting, and habitat destruction. Feral dogs and cats disturb nesting sites, further negatively impacting the species. However, overhunting is widely considered as the greatest threat to the species.
Throughout the twentieth century half a million to 800,000 copper pheasants were killed by hunters every year. In 1976, to prevent further decline of the species, it was made illegal in Japan to hunt female copper pheasants.  The number of birds hunted decreased to 100,000 per year by the 1990s. As of 2006, the number of birds killed annually had dropped to 6,000. To allow for hunting, captive-bred copper pheasants are released every year throughout Japan, including in regions such as Hokkaido and Sado Island where they are a non-native species.


References


Bibliography
Beebe, Williams (1922). A Monograph of the Pheasants (PDF). Vol. 3. London, England: HF & G Witherby.
Beolens, Bo; Watkins, Michael; Grayson, Michael (2014). The Eponym Dictionary of Birds. Bloomsbury Publishing. ISBN 978-1-4729-0574-1.
BirdLife International (2016). "Syrmaticus soemmerringii". The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species (Report). doi:10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22679340A92811551.en.
Erickson, Jens; Erickson, Hanne (1988). Collect Birds on Stamps (2 ed.). London and Ringwood: Stanley Gibbons. ISBN 0-85259-182-9.
Johnsgard, Paul A. (1999). The pheasants of the world: biology and natural history (2nd ed.). Washington, DC: Smithsonian Institution Press. p. 271. ISBN 978-1-56098-839-7.
Mahood, D.; Benstead, P.; Taylor, J. (2024). "Copper Pheasant (Syrmaticus soemmerringii) - BirdLife species factsheet". datazone.birdlife.org. Retrieved 2024-05-11.
McGowan, Philip J. K.; Garson, Peter J. (1995). Pheasants: status survey and conservation action plan, 1995-1999 (PDF). WPA/BirdLife/SSC Pheasant Specialist Group, International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources. Gland, Switzerland: IUCN. ISBN 978-2-8317-0299-5.
Watanabe, Junya; Matsuoka, Hiroshige; Hasegawa, Yoshikazu (2018-08-11). "Pleistocene non-passeriform landbirds from Shiriya, northeast Japan" (PDF). Acta Palaeontologica Polonica. 63 (3): 469–491. doi:10.4202/app.00509.2018. ISSN 0078-8562.

}}The '''copper pheasant''' ('''''Syrmaticus soemmerringii'''''), also known as '''Soemmerring's pheasant''' or {{nihongo|'''yamadori'''|ヤマドリ|}},'''{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=269}}'''  is a pheasant [[endemism|endemic]] to the [[Japanese archipelago]]. The scientific name commemorates the [[Germany|German]] scientist [[Samuel Thomas von Sömmerring]]. It is the official bird of multiple [[Prefectures of Japan|Japanese prefectures]], cities, and towns. It was commonly hunted for sport throughout the 20th century.

Its population has been in consistent decline since the 1970s due to factors including [[habitat destruction]] and predation, but the most widely cited cause is overhunting. 

==Taxonomy==
[[File:BirdsAsiaJohnGoVIIGoul 0160.jpg|thumb|Illustration from [[John Gould|John Gould's]] ''Birds of Asia'']]
The copper pheasant was described in 1830 by [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|Coenraad Temmerick]]{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=269}} as ''Phasianus'' ''soemmerringii.''<ref>{{Cite book |last=Temminck |first=Coenraad J. |author-link=Coenraad Jacob Temminck |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/109466 |title=Nouveau recueil de planches coloriées d'oiseaux : pour servir de suite et de complément aux planches enluminées de Buffon |year=1838 |location=Paris |page=83 |language=fr |trans-title=New collection of colored plates of birds, to serve as a continuation and complement to Buffon's illuminated plates}}</ref> It was named for the [[Germany|German]] scientist [[Samuel Thomas von Sömmerring]],{{Sfn|Beolens|Watkins|Grayson|2014|p=520}} while the subspecies ''S. s. ijimae'' is named for Japanese zoologist [[Isao Ijima]].{{Sfn|Beolens|Watkins|Grayson|2014|pp=274-275}} It was reclassified into the ''Syrmaticus'' genus in 1914 by [[William Beebe]], despite the differences between the males, because he saw a high number of similarities between females of the genus.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Beebe |first=William |author-link=William Beebe |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_fEYAAAAYAAJ |title=Preliminary Pheasant Studies |year=1914 |location= United States |pages=283–284}}</ref> However, based on the reports of interbreeding between the copper pheasant and the [[green pheasant]] (''Phasianus versicolor'') in [[Nobusuke Takatsukasa|Nobusuke Takatsukasa's]] 1943 pamphlet, ''Studies of the Galli of Nippon'',  the [[Ornithological Society of Japan]] moved the bird back into the ''Phasianus'' genus.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bohl |first=Wayne H. |url=https://archive.org/details/specialscientif00unitct/page/46/mode/2up?q=copper |title=A Study and Review of the Japanese Green and Korean Ring-necked Pheasants |publisher=[[United States Department of the Interior]] |year=1964 |location=Washington D.C. |pages=29}}</ref> In the same book, Takatsukasa also split the copper pheasant into two species and eliminated all subspecies. Those, as well as other taxonomic changes he proposed, were termed by a [[United States Fish and Wildlife Service]] report as "not in accordance with sound systematic practice".<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Austin |first1=O.L |url=https://archive.org/details/japaneseornithol305aust |title=Japanese Ornithology and Mammalogy during World War II (An Annotated Bibliography): Wildlife Leaflet 305 |last2=Hachisuka |first2=Masauji |last3=Takashima |first3=Haruo |last4=Kuroda |first4=Nagahisa |publisher=[[United States Department of the Interior]], [[United States Fish and Wildlife Service]] |year=1948 |page=15}}</ref> The copper pheasant is sometimes still referred to as ''Phasianus'' ''soemmerringii'' in Japanese literature.{{Sfn|Watanabe|Matsuoka|Hasegawa|2018|p=475}}

There are five recognized subspecies of copper pheasant:<ref>{{Cite web |title=Syrmaticus soemmerringii |url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=176097#null |access-date=2024-05-21 |website=Integrated Taxonomic Information System |language=en-US}}</ref>
* '''Soermmering's copper pheasant''' (''S. s. soemmerringii'') <small>(Temminck, 1830)</small> is found the northern and central [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":0" /> The [[nominate subspecies]], it is dark reddish in colour with an amber sheen to the rump and tips of the upper-tail coverts.
* '''Ijima copper pheasant''' (''S. s. ijimae'') <small>([[Henry Eeles Dresser|Dresser]], 1902)</small>, also known as the {{nihongo|'''koshijiro-yamadori'''|コシジロヤマドリ|}}<ref name=":4" /> (literally ''white-waisted yamadori''), is found in southern to central Kyushu.<ref name=":0" /> It is similar in appearance to ''soemmeringii'', but the male has a white rump.
* '''Scintillating copper pheasant''' (''S. s. scintillans'') <small>([[John Gould|Gould]], 1866)</small>, also known as the '''shining copper pheasant''' or Honda copper pheasant'''{{Sfn|Beebe|1922|p=162}} '''is found in northern [[Honshu]], from the [[Kansai region]] to [[Aomori Prefecture|Aomori prefecture]].<ref name=":0" /> It is the palest subspecies, with broad white fringes on its belly and flanks.
* '''Shikoku copper pheasant''' (''S. s. intermedius'') (<small>[[Nagamichi Kuroda|Kuroda]], 1919)</small> is found in [[Shikoku]] and the [[Chūgoku region]]s.<ref name=":0" /> Like ''scintillans'', it has white fringes on its belly and flanks, but is darker.
* '''Pacific copper pheasant''' (''S. s. subrufus'') (<small>Kuroda, 1919)</small> is found in the [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime]], and [[Kōchi Prefecture|Kōchi]], and [[Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi]] prefectures and the [[Bōsō Peninsula|Bōsō]], [[Izu Peninsula|Izu]], and [[Kii Peninsula|Kii peninsula]].<ref name=":0" /> It has golden fringes on its rump and upper-tail coverts.

Both ''S. s. ijimae''{{Sfn|Beebe|1922|p=175}} and ''S. s. scintillans'' were initially described as distinct species.{{Sfn|Beebe|1922|p=169}}

Pleistocene-era ''Syrmaticus'' fossils in Japan have been classified as ''S. soemmerringii'', but this identification is disputed.{{Sfn|Watanabe|Matsuoka|Hasegawa|2018|pp=474-475}}

==Description==
It is a large [[pheasant]] with a rich [[copper]]y chestnut plumage, yellowish [[Beak|bill]], brown [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]], and red facial skin. The female is a brown bird with greyish brown upperparts and buff barred dark brown below. The male has short spurs on its grey legs, while the female has none. Males are between {{Convert|87.5|cm|in}} and {{Convert|136|cm|in}} long, including the tail, while the female is between {{Convert|51|cm|in}} and {{Convert|54|cm|in}} (subspecies scintillating copper pheasant, ''scintillans'').<ref>''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'', Volume 2, [[Lynx Edicions]], [[Barcelona]]</ref> Juveniles have similar plumage to the females.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=McGowan |first1=Phil |title=Pheasants, Partridges & Grouse: Including buttonquails, sandgrouse and allies |last2=Madge |first2=Steve |publisher=[[A & C Black]] |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-4081-3566-2 |edition=illustrated |pages=320–321}}</ref> The plumage of the chicks is, overall, chestnut, with some darker patches of brown on the back of the neck and cream-coloured feathers on the face. Darker stripes of cream and dark brown run along their face and neck.{{Sfn|Beebe|1922|p=161}}

==Distribution and habitat==
The copper pheasant is distributed in and [[Endemism in birds|endemic]] to the hill and mountain forests of the [[Honshū]], [[Kyūshū]], and [[Shikoku]] islands{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=269}} at elevations of up to {{Convert|1800|m|mi}}.{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}} It typically confines itself to deciduous forest floors with extensive vegetation. However, members of the species also lives in conifer forests. They often nest on the edges of grasslands.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Kawaji |first=Noritomo |date=2013-11-28 |title=Copper Pheasant |url=https://www.bird-research.jp/1_shiryo/seitai/yamadori.pdf |journal=Bird Research News |volume=3 |issue=11 |pages=4–5 |access-date=2024-05-09}}</ref> It naturally occurs only south of the [[Blakiston's Line]], and this species' distribution was originally cited as evidence for the line's existence.{{Sfn|Watanabe|Matsuoka|Hasegawa|2018|p=485}}

Copper pheasant populations have been established on [[Hokkaido]] and [[Sado Island]].<ref name=":0" />

== Behavior and ecology ==
The bird is not heavily vocal, only occasionally making a "kuk-kuk"-like call when breeding or threatened.<ref name=":0" />{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=271}} These pheasants are known to sunbathe, especially when living in grasslands. They roost in trees,<ref name=":0" /> and are a [[non-migratory]] bird.{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}}

=== Breeding ===
[[File:Syrmaticus soemmerringii ssp. subrufus chick.jpg|alt=Pheasant chick running through undergrowth|thumb|Juvenile Syrmaticus soemmerringii ssp. subrufus]]
The copper pheasant's mating season occurs between March and July, with the exact month depending local climate. During this time, males perform their wing-whirring display{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=271}} and fight other males in an attempt to secure a mate. It is unknown if the species is monogamous.<ref name=":0" /> Male copper pheasants exhibit aggressive behaviour in captivity, sometimes killing their mates.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Delacour |first=Jean |url=https://archive.org/details/pheasantbreeding0005dela |title=Pheasant breeding and care |publisher=[[T.F.H. Publications]] |year=1978 |location=Neptune, New Jersey |pages=28–29}}</ref> It is usual for this species to be bred in captivity by means of artificial insemination.<ref name=":5" />

Copper pheasants typically nest on the ground, but have also been recorded nesting in trees.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kawaji |first=Noritomo |date=2006 |title=Yamadori chijō negura no hatsu kansatsu kiroku |script-title=ja:ヤマドリ地上ねぐらの初観察記録 |trans-title=First observation of ground roosting of the copper pheasant. |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jjo/55/2/55_2_92/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=日本鳥学会誌 |volume=55 |issue=2 |pages=92–95 |doi=10.2326/jjo.55.92|url-access=subscription }}</ref> They often build their nests in hollows sheltered by fallen trees.{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=271}}<ref name=":0" /> Captive birds have a clutch of ten to twenty eggs, while wild birds have a clutch of seven to thirteen eggs.{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=271}} The cream-coloured<ref name=":2" /> or tan<ref name=":0" /> eggs take twenty five days to hatch,<ref name=":5">{{Cite book |last=Howman |first=Keith |url=https://archive.org/details/pheasantsofworld0000keit |title=Pheasants of the World: Their Breeding and Management |publisher=Hancock House Publishing |year=1993 |page=136 |quote=Status in captivity: poor outside Japan}}</ref> and the chicks are able to walk within hours of hatching. After all the chicks have hatched, they abandon the nest and stay at their mother's side until adulthood. Occasionally, the male pheasant is seen near his offspring and mate,{{Sfn|Beebe|1922|pp=166-167}} but young are raised primarily by their mother. When threatened, she will either attack potential predators or attempt to distract them by feigning injury herself.<ref name=":0" /> Young birds reach maturity in less than a year.{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=271}}

=== Food and feeding ===
Their diet consists mainly of insects, insect larvae, worms, crabs, berries, and acorns, especially those from the ''[[Castanopsis]]'', ''[[Machilus]]'', ''[[Cleyera]]'', and ''[[Chestnut|Castanea]]'' trees.{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=270}} Adults eat a substantial amount of leaf and fern matter,<ref name=":0" />  while the young mostly consume insects.<ref name=":0" />{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=270}}

=== Threats ===
The copper pheasant serves as an important source of prey to Japanese populations of the [[golden eagle]] and [[Hodgson's hawk eagle]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Yamaguchi |first1=Yasuhiro |last2=Kawaji |first2=Noritomo |year=2003 |title=Habitat Use of the Copper Pheasant Syrmaticus soemmerringii in Central Japan |url=https://www.ffpri.affrc.go.jp/labs/kanko/388-5.pdf |journal=Bulletin of FFPRI |publisher=Forestry and Forest Products Research Institute |volume=2 |issue=3 |page=193}}</ref>  It is susceptible to infection by the [[heterakis gallinarum]] parasite.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Parasitic Diseases of Wild Birds |date=2009-03-10 |publisher=[[Wiley (publisher)|Wiley]] |isbn=978-0-8138-0457-6 |editor-last=Atkinson |editor-first=Carter T. |edition=2009 |page=391 |editor-last2=Hunter |editor-first2=D. Bruce |editor-last3=Thomas |editor-first3=Nancy J.}}</ref>

== Relationship to humans ==

=== In Japan ===
[[File:Ehon Momochidori Kyōka-awase-百千鳥狂歌合-Myriad Birds- Picture Book of Playful Verse (Momo chidori kyōka-awase) MET DP283344.jpg|thumb|1791 woodblock of a copper pheasant by [[Kitagawa Utamaro]].]]
The copper pheasant can take the role of a [[yōkai]] in Japanese folklore. Tying the pheasant's tail feather's to an arrow grants the arrow the ability to harm malignant spirits in some stories.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Aramata |first=Hiroshi |title=Sekai dai hakubutsu zukan |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1987 |volume=4 |page=133 |language=ja |script-title=ja:世界大博物図鑑 |trans-title=World Museum Illustrated Book}}</ref>

The copper pheasant is used in an early 8th century poem by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]],<ref name="mostow">{{cite book | last = Mostow | first = Joshua S. | title = Pictures of the Heart: The Hyakunin Isshu in Word and Image | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1996 | isbn = 0-8248-1705-2 | page=149}}</ref> where it is used a metaphor for "the separation of lovers".<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mewhinney |first=Matthew1 |year=2022 |title=The Pheasant's Call and the Sound of Sympathy. |url=https://jll.pitt.edu/ojs/JLL/article/view/223 |journal=Japanese Language & Literature |volume=56 |issue=1 |pages=17–18 |doi=10.5195/jll.2022.223 |issn=1536-7827|doi-access=free }}{{Creative Commons text attribution notice|cc=by4|from this source=yes}}</ref>

{{Waka|葦引の山鳥の尾のしだり尾の|ながながし夜をひとりかも寝む|reading=ashihiki no yamadori no o no shidari o no naganaga shi yo o hitori kamo nemu|translation=Am I to sleep alone this long night, long like a drooping tail, tail feathers of copper pheasants resting mountains apart?|source=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}

Several screens, dating to the eighth century and incorporating copper pheasant feathers, are kept in the [[Shōsōin|Shōsō-in]] temple. The screens were mistakenly assumed to be Chinese, but are, in reality, of Japanese origin.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Milburn |first=Olivia |date=2020 |title=Featherwork in Early and Medieval China |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.7817/jameroriesoci.140.3.0549 |journal=[[Journal of the American Oriental Society]] |language=en |volume=140 |issue=3 |pages=561 |doi=10.7817/jameroriesoci.140.3.0549 |jstor=10.7817/jameroriesoci.140.3.0549 |issn=2169-2289 }}</ref> Unidentified bones of either copper pheasants or chickens have been found at Japanese archaeological sites from the [[Kofun period]].<ref>{{Cite book |title=The Oxford Handbook of Zooarchaeology |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-19-968647-6 |editor-last=Russ |editor-first=Hannah |page=345 |editor-last2=Vickers |editor-first2=Kim |editor-last3=Rizzetto |editor-first3=Mauro |editor-last4=Viner-Daniels |editor-first4=Sarah |editor-last5=Albarella |editor-first5=Umberto}}</ref>

The copper pheasant is a popular [[Game (hunting)|game animal]] in Japan.{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=272}} They are hunted both as a source of food and a source of [[Field sports|entertainment]].{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}}

It is the official bird of the [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ken no shiboru |script-title=ja:県のシンボル |trans-title=Prefecture Symbols |url=https://www.pref.gunma.jp/01/bu0100008.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220921013035/https://www.pref.gunma.jp/01/bu0100008.html |archive-date=2022-09-21 |access-date=2024-05-09 |website=www.pref.gunma.jp |language=ja}}</ref> and [[Akita Prefecture|Akita]] prefectures.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2017-10-02 |title=Akita no shinboru (Kiddzupēji) |script-title=ja:秋田のシンボル(キッズページ) |trans-title=Symbols of the It has been depicted on stamps from Japan{{Sfn|Erickson|Erickson|1988|pp=140-143}} and Laos.{{Sfn|Erickson|Erickson|1988|p=154}}

=== Outside Japan ===
Outside of Japan, the copper pheasant is infrequently<ref name=":5" /> kept as a pet or [[Animal fancy|display animal]].{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}}

The [[Importation of Plumage (No. 1) Order 1922]] ([[SR&O 1922]]/275) added an addendum to the [[Importation of Plumage (Prohibition) Act 1921]] ([[11 & 12 Geo. 5]]. c. 16) which halted the importation of copper pheasant feathers into the UK. They were removed from the list after a year by the [[Importation of Plumage (No. 2) Order 1923]] ([[SR&O 1923]]/1496).<ref>{{Cite news |date=1923-08-20 |title=The Plumage Act. |url= |work=[[The Times]] |location=London, England |page=7 |issue=43424}}</ref>

From 1907 to 1914, there was a series unsuccessful attempts made by the Hawaii Board of Agriculture and Forestry to establish copper pheasants colonies on the [[Hawaiian Islands|Hawaiian islands]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schwartz |first1=Charles W. |last2=Schwartz |first2=Elizabeth Reeder |year=1951 |title=An Ecological Reconnaissance of the Pheasants in Hawaii |url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/68/3/281-314/5223856 |journal=[[The Auk]] |volume=68 |issue=3 |pages=283 |doi=10.2307/4080980|jstor=4080980 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last1=Pyle |first1=Robert L. |last2=Pyle |first2=Peter |date=2017-01-01 |title=The Birds of the Hawaiian Islands: Occurrence, History, Distribution, and Status |url=http://hbs.bishopmuseum.org/birds/rlp-monograph/NonEstablished.htm |access-date=2024-05-12 |website=hbs.bishopmuseum.org}}</ref> Another attempt to introduce the birds to the United States was made around 1885 by an American diplomat, when he released three pairs of birds into [[Puget Sound]].<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=Phillips |first=John C. |url=https://archive.org/details/WildBirdsIntroducedOrTransplantedInNorthAmerica |title=Wild birds introduced or transplanted in North America |publisher=[[United States Department of Agriculture]] |year=1928 |page=45}}</ref> It was made illegal in the U.S. states of Nebraska<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/gamelawsinnebras00nebr/page/n3/mode/2up |title=Game Laws of Nebraska in force March 7, 1899 |publisher=Jacob Nord & Co., Printers |year=1898 |editor-last=Sheldon |editor-first=A. E. |pages=1}}</ref> and Oregon<ref>{{Cite book |last=Woodcock |first=Arthur Roy |url=https://archive.org/details/annotatedlistbi00woodgoog |title=Annotated list of the birds of Oregon |publisher=Oregon Agricultural Printing Office |year=1902 |pages=104}}</ref> to hunt birds he had introduced, but a copper pheasant population was never established.<ref name=":3" />

== Status ==
As of 2025, the copper pheasant was listed as "[[Least concern species|Least Concern]]" on the [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species, with an estimated population of 50,000 – 300,000 mature individuals.<ref name="iucn status"/> Prior to this, the species had been listed as [[Near Threatened]] since 2004.<ref name="iucn status"/> The ''ijimae'' subspecies is protected in Japan,{{Sfn|McGowan|Garson|1995|p=50}} with both ''S. s. soemmeringii'' and ''S. s. ijimae'' being listed as "Near Threatened" on the [[Japanese Red List]].<ref>{{Cite web |year=2020 |title=[Honyūrui] Kankyōshō reddo risuto 2020 |script-title=ja:【哺乳類】環境省レッドリスト2020 |trans-title=(Mammals) Ministry of the Environment Red List 2020 |url=https://www.env.go.jp/content/900515981.pdf |access-date=2024-05-11 |website=www.env.go.jp |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |language=ja}}</ref>

In 1995, an IUCN report called for the continued monitoring of the species, citing over-hunting and a lack of knowledge about the species' behaviour as a reason to study and protect them.{{Sfn|McGowan|Garson|1995|p=97}} Threats to the species include non-native predation, overhunting, and habitat destruction.{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}} Feral dogs and cats disturb nesting sites, further negatively impacting the species.{{Sfn|McGowan|Garson|1995|p=8}} However, overhunting is widely considered as the greatest threat to the species.{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}}{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=272}}<ref>{{Cite book |last=Richardson |first=Matthew |title=Threatened and Recently Extinct Vertebrates of the World |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=2023 |isbn=978-1-108-85258-6}}</ref>

Throughout the twentieth century half a million{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=272}}{{Sfn|McGowan|Garson|1995|p=50}} to 800,000{{Sfn|Mahood|Benstead|Taylor|5=2024}} copper pheasants were killed by hunters every year. In 1976, to prevent further decline of the species, it was made illegal in Japan to hunt female copper pheasants.{{Sfn|Johnsgard|1999|p=272}}{{Sfn|BirdLife International|2016}}  The number of birds hunted decreased to 100,000 per year by the 1990s.{{Sfn|Mahood|Benstead|Taylor|5=2024}} As of 2006, the number of birds killed annually had dropped to 6,000.<ref name=":1" /> To allow for hunting, captive-bred copper pheasants are released every year throughout Japan,{{Sfn|McGowan|Garson|1995|p=50}} including in regions such as [[Hokkaido]] and [[Sado Island]] where they are a non-native species.<ref name=":0" />
